 1.0 Introduction 

This document provides basic information for using the JPL MEaSUREs-WVTC (Water Vapor, 
temperature and Clouds) Infrared (IR) sounder, microwave (MW) sounder, and narrowband 
imager matchup index dataset for Aqua, SNPP, and JPSS satellites. These data are intended to 
simplify the comparison and analysis of sounder and imager observations.  Sounder views are 
typically 15 km wide at nadir (for IR) and 50 km wide at nadir (for microwave; MW), while 
imager pixels are roughly 1 km wide, so each sounder view includes hundreds of imager pixels.  
The data sets described here match sounder view indices in digital files to imager indices in 
different sets of digital files. The matched index files follow sounder data convention of 
granules defined as six minutes in time, giving 10 granules per hour and 240 per day.  The 
resulting files point from a sounder view to the indices of all collocated imager pixels in one or 
two imager files.  (Sounder and imager files were not coordinate at time of design, so sounders 
and imagers have different granule sizes.) Relating imager indices to sounder indices is 
straightforward because the observations are essentially simultaneous, so searches are limited 
to one or two six minute granules. The basic methodology uses pointing information to related 
sounder and imager views (Wang et al., 2013; Yue et al. 2022) 

We describe here sounder-imager matches for three sets of instruments, each set carried on a 
separate spacecraft.  They are:  AIRS/AMSU sounders matched to the MODIS imager on Aqua, 
CrIS/ATMS sounder to VIIRS  imager on Suomi NPP, and CrIS/ATMS to VIIRS on JPSS-1, and 
future JPSS satellites. 

1.1 Dataset/Mission Instrument Description 

Measurements and retrievals from the NASA’s Earth Observing System (EOS) satellites, including 
Terra  and  Aqua,  the joint  NASA/NOAA  Suomi  National  Polar-orbiting  Partnership (SNPP),  and 
NOAA’s new generation of Joint Polar Satellite System (JPSS) series weather satellites, are a key 
component  in  the  Program  of  Record  (POR)  for  atmospheric  water  vapor,  temperature,  and 
cloud  properties.  Because  of  efforts  to  maintain  continuity  and  consistency  by  rigorous 
instrument  mission  design  and  ongoing  algorithm  development,  these  satellites  provide  high 
quality, long-term data records derived from the Top of Atmosphere (TOA) radiances observed 
across a wide range of the emission and reflection spectrum. Particularly, Aqua, SNPP, and JPSS-
1 (now NOAA-20), which were launched in 2002, 2011, and 2016, respectively, carry high spatial 
resolution  narrowband  imagers,  hyperspectral  infrared  (IR)  and  microwave  (MW)  sounding 
instruments. As a result, observations with similar spatial resolution and coverage, and similar 
spectral resolution at analogous wavelengths are obtained from different satellites. For  Aqua, 
this  instrument  trio  consists  of  the  Atmospheric  Infrared  Sounder  (AIRS),  the  Advanced 
Microwave  Sounding  Unit  (AMSU),  and  the  Moderate  Resolution  Imaging  Spectroradiometer 
(MODIS).  For  SNPP  and  JPSS,  the  trio  includes  the  Cross-track  Infrared  Sounder  (CrIS),  the 
Advanced Technology Microwave Sounder (ATMS), and the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer 
Suite (VIIRS).  

5 

 
 
 
 The  sounder-imager  combination  on  the  same  sun-synchronous  polar-orbiting  satellite, 
together  with  the  coverage  overlap  between  satellites,  provides  opportunities  for  utilizing 
spectral and spatial capabilities from different sensors at global scale. To facilitate the utilization 
of multi-sensor observations at pixel scale, the WVTC sounder-imager matchup index products 
are generated for various polar orbiting environmental satellites using the same algorithm with 
the consistent data formatting. These products include AIRS and MODIS on Aqua, CrIS and VIIRS 
on SNPP and JPSS-1.  Future JPSS satellites will have similar instrument suites to those on SNPP 
and JPSS-1. 

1.1.1 AIRS/AMSU and MODIS 

AIRS is a continuously operating cross-track scanning sounder, consisting of a telescope that 
feeds an echelle spectrometer.  The AIRS infrared spectrometer acquires 2378 spectral samples 
at resolutions, λ/Δλ, ranging from 1086 to 1570,  in three bands: 3.74 to 4.61 µm, 6.20 to 8.22 
µm, and 8.8 to 15.4 µm.  The angular extent of the infrared channels is 1.1° in diameter, which 
corresponds to a horizontal footprint of about 15x15 km in the nadir. 

AMSU-A is a 15-channel microwave temperature sounder implemented as two independently 
operated modules. Module 1 (AMSU-A1) has 12 channels in the 50-58 GHz oxygen absorption 
band which provide the primary temperature sounding capabilities and 1 channel at 89 GHz 
which provides surface and moisture information.  Module 2 (AMSU-A2) has 2 channels: one at 
23.8 GHz and one at 31.4 GHz. These respectively provide surface and moisture information 
(total precipitable water and cloud liquid water).  Like AIRS, AMSU-A is a cross-track scanner.  
AMSU-A scans once per 8 seconds, 1/3 the rate of AIRS, and its footprints are approximately 
three times as large as those of AIRS (45 km at nadir).  This results in three AIRS scans per 
AMSU-A scans and nine AIRS footprints per AMSU-A footprint.  Each AIRS/AMSU granule 
includes six minutes of scanning (240 granules per day), and contains 30 x 45 AMSU and 90 x 
135 AIRS footprints crosstrack x alongtrack. 

AIRS and AMSU are on the Aqua spacecraft, in a sun-synchronous orbit with local equator 
crossing times of 1:30 PM (northward moving orbit) and 1:30 AM. 

The moderate-resolution imaging spectroradiometer (MODIS) is a payload scientific instrument 
built by Santa Barbara Remote Sensing that was launched into Earth orbit by NASA in 1999 on 
board the Terra (EOS AM) Satellite, and in 2002 on board the Aqua (EOS PM) satellite. The 
instruments capture data in 36 spectral bands ranging in wavelength from 0.4 µm to 14.4 µm 
and at varying horizontal resolutions (2 bands at 250 m, 5 bands at 500 m and 29 bands at 
1 km), roughly an order of magnitude smaller than the AIRS or AMSU resolutions. Each MODIS 
instrument images the entire Earth every 1 to 2 days. They are designed to provide 
measurements in large-scale global dynamics including changes in Earth's cloud cover, radiation 
budget and processes occurring in the oceans, on land, and in the lower atmosphere.  The 
swaths of Aqua MODIS are slightly wider than AIRS/AMSU swaths, so all AIRS/AMSU fields of 
view are completely covered by Aqua MODIS observations. 

6 

 
 
 
 1.1.2 CrIS/ATMS and VIIRS 

CrIS, ATMS, and VIIRS are three of the key instruments carried on SNPP and JPSS satellites. CrIS 
is a Michelson interferometer. The scan mirror stepwise “stares” at the Earth in the cross-track 
direction from −48.3° to +48.3 ° from nadir with a 3.3° step angle, equaling a 2200 km swath 
width on the Earth. Nine field stops define the 3 × 3 detector array for each IR wavelength 
band, which are in a 3 × 3 arrays of 0.963° circles and separated by 1.1°. The CrIS radiance 
spectrum (without apodization) covers three IR bands (see below) with spectral resolutions of 
0.625 cm−1, 1.25 cm−1, and 2.5 cm−1 at the normal operational mode (a total of 1305 spectral 
channels). After apodization by using a Hamming function, the effective spectral resolution 
decreases by a factor of 1.82. CrIS has 1305 spectral channels over three wavelength bands: 
long-wave infrared (LWIR) (9.14–15.38 µm), middle-wave IR (MWIR) (5.71–8.26 µm), and short-
wave IR (SWIR) (3.92–4.64 µm). The sounder instrument CrIS provides information on the 
vertical profiles of temperature, water vapor, and critical trace gases of the atmosphere, with 
spatial resolution comparable to AIRS (~14.0 km at nadir).  

ATMS is a cross-track scanning microwave radiometer with channels specified to provide 
essentially the same data products as the prior AMSU-A and Microwave Humidity Sounder 
(MHS) instruments (primarily atmospheric temperature and humidity sounding) but with 
several key enhancements including additional sounding channels, wider swath width and 
better noise equivalent delta temperature (NDΔT) than AMSU-A, etc. The ATMS sensor 
provides 96 FOVs per scan sweep covering the frequency range of 23.8 GHz to 190 GHz in 22 
channels. ATMS works in conjunction with CrIS to form the Cross-Track Infrared and Microwave 
Sounder Suite (CrIMSS), which provides the capability of all-sky atmospheric sounding. 

VIIRS is a whiskbroom scanning imaging radiometer with a field of regard of ±56.3° in the cross-
track direction. The swath width is 3060 km. VIIRS has 22 spectral bands covering the spectrum 
between 0.412 µm and 12.01 µm, including 16 moderate resolution bands (M-bands) with a 
spatial resolution of 750 m at nadir, 5 imaging resolution bands (I-bands)–375 m at nadir, and 1 
panchromatic Day-Night-Band (DNB) with 750 m spatial resolution throughout the scan. VIIRS 
uses a unique approach of pixel aggregation which controls the pixel growth towards the end of 
the scan. As a result, the VIIRS spatial resolutions for nadir and edge-of-scan data are 
comparable. To save transmission bandwidth, VIIRS also uses a “bow-tie removal” approach 
that removes duplicate pixels in the off-nadir areas where there is an overlap of several pixels 
between adjacent scans.  

1.2 Algorithm Background 

Utilizing the multi-sensor capability at the pixel scale requires accurate and computationally 
efficient collocation of sounder and imager measurements. Various collocation methods exist 
(Schreier et al. 2010, Nagle and Holz 2009, Yue et al. 2013). For the data sets described here, 

7 

 
 
 
 
 the method developed by Wang et al. (2016) is applied by matching the instantaneous multi-
sensor observations directly based on line-of-sight (LOS) pointing vectors, defined as the vector 
from the satellite position to the Earth surface pixel location. The details of this method and its 
accuracy are discussed at length in Wang et al. (2016) for VIIRS and CrIS, which are further 
expanded to be applied to collocations between MODIS and AIRS on Aqua by Yue et al. (2022).  
The matchup indices are generated by applying the same collocation method to different polar 
orbiting satellites to match the finer resolution imager pixels (MODIS and VIIRS) within a given 
sounder FOV (AIRS and CrIS). The LOS vectors are calculated using the geolocation datasets for 
different sensors, which contain latitude, longitude, satellite range, satellite azimuth and zenith 
angles.  
Collocation is performed using the criterion that the angular difference between the LOS 
vectors for sounder and imager should be less than half of the sounder FOV size angle. The CrIS 
FOV is treated as a 0.963° circle which corresponds to ~41% of the peak response and collects 
~98% of total radiation falling on the detector (Wang et al. 2013). AIRS has a FOV half-power 
width of 1.1° (Fishbein et al. 2001), so 1.1° is used for AIRS in the collocation. Figure 1 shows an 
example of the results. Indices of MODIS 1 km and VIIRS 750 m resolutions are reported. 

Due to the slight time coverage difference between the sounder and imager granules, an 
elastic search is performed based on the time frame of each sounder granule and all imager 
granules that have overlap observation time windows. The corresponding imager granules are 
then concatenated along the ground track of the satellite. The latitude and longitude boundary 
boxes, the observation time, and the granule names of sounder and imager are all given as 
global attributes in the index products. The imager cross-track and along-track indices that are 
within each sounder FOV are saved as variables following the naming convention of VIIRS 
(MODIS) and CrIS dimension list after concatenation of imager granules:  

●  Number_of_pixels (cross-track, corresponding to MODIS Cell_Across_Swath_1km) 
●  Number_of_lines (along-track, corresponding to MODIS Cell_Along_Swath_1km) 

1.3 Constituent Data Sets 

For detailed information on the AMSU/AIRS data, consult the documentation located at 
https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/information/documents?title=AIRS%20Documentation. 

For detailed information on the CrIS/ATMS data, consult the documentation located 
athttps://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/datasets?keywords=CrIS&page=1&project=SUOMI-NPP,NPP-
JPSS,JPSS. 

 For detailed information on the collection 5/6/6.1 MODIS Standard Products including the 1-
km geolocation product, consult the documentation located at https://atmosphere-
imager.gsfc.nasa.gov/products. 

8 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 For detailed information on the VIIRS Moderate Resolution geolocation data , consult the 
documentation located at: 

For SNPP-VIIRS:  https://ladsweb.modaps.eosdis.nasa.gov/missions-and-
measurements/products/VNP03MOD/. 
For JPSS1-VIIRS: https://ladsweb.modaps.eosdis.nasa.gov/missions-and-
measurements/products/VJ103MOD/. 

For information and access tools pertaining to NetCDF, consult the documentation at 
http://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/.

2.0 Data Organization 

The data consists of: 

1)  The WVTC SNPP CrIS and VIIRS 750m matchup index files in NetCDF4 format 

●  ShortName = SNPP_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND 
●  LongName =  SNPP CrIS-VIIRS 750-m Matchup Indexes V1 
●  VersionID =   1 
●  DOI = 10.5067/MEASURES/WVCC/DATA211 
●  DOI_Authority = http://dx.doi.org/ 

2)  The WVTC Aqua AIRS-MODIS1km matchup index files in NetCDF4 format 

●  ShortName = Aqua_AIRS_MODIS1km_IND 
●  LongName =  Aqua AIRS-MODIS 1-km Matchup Indexes V1 
●  VersionID =   1 
●  DOI = 10.5067/MEASURES/WVCC/DATA210 
●  DOI_Authority = http://dx.doi.org/ 

3)  The WVTC JPSS-1 (NOAA-20) CrIS and VIIRS 750m matchup index files in NetCDF4 

format 

10 

 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 ●  ShortName = J1_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND 
●  LongName =  JPSS-1 CrIS-VIIRS 750-m Matchup Indexes V1 
●  VersionID =   1 
●  DOI = 10.5067/MEASURES/WVCC/DATA212 
●  DOI_Authority = http://dx.doi.org/ 

The index product follows the granularity of the sounder, i.e., each file contains the matchup 
imager indices for one sounder granule. Within each file, Imager indices are reported on the 
sounder resolution and dimension. For AIRS, it is 90 scans by 135 scan lines. For CrIS, it is 9 fovs 
by 30 scans by 45 scan lines. All files are in NetCDF4 format. The content and dimension of the 
products are shown with the two examples for CrIS and VIIRS, and AIRS and MODIS, 
respectively. 

2.1 File Naming Convention 

2.1.1 SNPP_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND 

The filename convention for an SNPP_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND granule is: 

IND_CrIS_VIIRSMOD_SNPP.YYYYMMDDTSSSS.gGGG.nc 

Where: 

●  YYYY = 4 digit year number [2001 - ] 
●  MM = 2 digit month number [01-12] 
●  DD = 2 digit day of month [01-31] 
●  SSSS = 4 digit UTC time of the sounder observations which follows NASA CrIS L1b and L2 

product naming convention 

●  GGG = 3 digit granule number of CrIS observation 

For example, the index file for CrIS Granule 162 on Nov. 02, 2015 is: 

IND_CrIS_VIIRSMOD_SNPP.20151102T1606.g162.nc 

2.1.2 J1_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND 

The filename convention for an J1_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND granule is: 

IND_CrIS_VIIRSMOD_J1.YYYYMMDDTSSSS.gGGG.nc 

11 

 
 
 
 
 
 Where: 

●  YYYY = 4 digit year number [2001 - ] 
●  MM = 2 digit month number [01-12] 
●  DD = 2 digit day of month [01-31] 
●  SSSS = 4 digit UTC time of the sounder observations which follows NASA CrIS L1b and L2 

product naming convention 

●  GGG = 3 digit granule number of CrIS observation 

For example, the index file for CrIS Granule 162 on Nov. 02, 2015 is: 

IND_CrIS_VIIRSMOD_J1.20151102T1606.g162.nc 

2.1.3 Aqua_AIRS_MODIS1km_IND 

The filename convention for an Aqua_AIRS_MODIS1km_IND granule is: 

IND_AIRS_MODIS1km_AIRS.YYYY.MM.DD.GGG.nc 

Where (following the NASA AIRS L1b and L2 product naming convention): 

●  YYYY = 4 digit year number [2001 - ] of the AIRS observation 
●  MM = 2 digit month number [01-12] of the AIRS observation 
●  DD = 2 digit day of month [01-31] of the AIRS observation 
●  GGG = 3 digit granule number of AIRS observation 

For example, the index file for AIRS Granule 162 on Nov. 02, 2015 is: 

IND_AIRS_MODIS1km_AIRS.2015.11.02.162.nc 

2.2 File Format and Structure 

2.2.1 SNPP_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND and J1_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND 

The SNPP_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND and J1_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND (matchup index file) data type is in 
NetCDF4 format (http://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/) and contains the CrIS and 
750m resolution VIIRS matchup indices onboard SNPP and JPSS-1 (NOAA-20), respectively.  An 

12 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 example ncdump from the following matchup file for SNPP, 
IND_CrIS_VIIRSMOD_SNDR.SNPP.20151102T1606.g115.nc, will serve to explain its format: 

{ 

} 

dimensions: 
        GranuleCount_ImagerPixel = 5629882 ; 
        sounder_atrack = 45 ; 
        sounder_xtrack = 30 ; 
        sounder_fov = 9 ; 
variables: 
        int number_of_pixels(GranuleCount_ImagerPixel) ; 
                number_of_pixels:long_name = "imager cross-track index" ; 
                number_of_pixels:units = "none" ; 
                number_of_pixels:var_desc = "imager cross-track index" ; 
        int FOVCount_ImagerPixel(sounder_atrack, sounder_xtrack, sounder_fov) ; 
                FOVCount_ImagerPixel:long_name = "count of imager pixels per sounder 
FOV" ; 
                FOVCount_ImagerPixel:units = "none" ; 
                FOVCount_ImagerPixel:var_desc = "count of imager pixels per sounder 
FOV" ; 
        int number_of_lines(GranuleCount_ImagerPixel) ; 
                number_of_lines:long_name = "imager along-track index after 
concatenating imager granules along track" ; 
                number_of_lines:units = "none" ; 
                number_of_lines:var_desc = "imager along-track index after 
concatenating imager granules along track" ; 

// global attributes: 
                :_NCProperties = "version=2,netcdf=4.7.4,hdf5=1.12.0," ; 
                string :description = "Version-1 SNPP VIIRS-CrIS collocation index 
product by the project of Multidecadal Satellite Record of Water Vapor, Temperature, 
and Clouds (PI: Eric Fetzer) funded by NASA’s Making Earth System Data Records for Use 
in Research Environments (MEaSUREs) Program following Wang et al. 
(2016, https://doi.org/10.3390/rs8010076) and Yue et al. 
(2022, https://doi.org/10.5194/amt-15-2099-2022)." ; 
                :VERSION = "1" ; 
                :SHORT_NAME = "SNPP_CrIS_VIIRS750m_IND" ; 
                :TITLE = "SNPP CrIS-VIIRS 750-m Matchup Indexes V1" ; 
                :IDENTIFIER_PRODUCT_DOI_AUTHORITY = "http://dx.doi.org/" ; 
                :IDENTIFIER_PRODUCT_DOI = "10.5067/MEASURES/WVCC/DATA211" ; 
                :PRODUCTIONDATE = "2022-04-20T02:54:09.544226" ; 
                :CRIS_FILE = 
"SNDR.SNPP.CRIS.20180301T1124.m06.g115.L1B.std.v02_05.G.180423025311.nc" ; 
                :VIIRS_FILE1 = "VNP03MOD.A2018060.1118.001.2018076133456.nc" ; 
                :VIIRS_FILE2 = "VNP03MOD.A2018060.1124.001.2018076133459.nc" ; 
                :VIIRS_FILE3 = "VNP03MOD.A2018060.1130.001.2018076135912.nc" ; 
                :VIIRS_FILES_COUNT = 3 ; 
                :RANGEBEGINNINGDATE = "2018-03-01" ; 
                :RANGEBEGINNINGTIME = "11:24:09" ; 
                :RANGEENDINGDATE = "2018-03-01" ; 
                :RANGEENDINGTIME = "11:30:07" ; 
                :NORTHBOUNDINGCOORDINATE = -16.4527721405029 ; 
                :SOUTHBOUNDINGCOORDINATE = -40.9573860168457 ; 
                :EASTBOUNDINGCOORDINATE = 49.6223068237305 ; 
                :WESTBOUNDINGCOORDINATE = 21.2559261322021 ; 
                :TIME_TOLERANCE = "900 seconds" ; 
                :DISTANCE_TOLERANCE = "CrIS FOV size angle 0.963 deg divided by 2" ; 

13 

  
 
 There should be 240 files per day, corresponding to 240 CrIS granules. Global attribute 
cris_file_name gives the L1b CrIS file that collocation is performed for. Global attribute VIIRS file 
names give the VIIRS moderate resolution geolocation product files that have overlapped 
observation time with the CrIS granule. The VIIRS granules are concatenated and cross-
track/along-track VIIRS indices are given based on the dimension of concatenated VIIRS 
granules. The DOIs are 10.5067/MEASURES/WVCC/DATA211 for SNPP, and 
10.5067/MEASURES/WVCC/DATA212 for JPSS-1. 

2.2.2 Aqua_AIRS_MODIS1km_IND 

The Aqua_AIRS_MODIS1km_IND (matchup index file) data type is in NetCDF4 format 
(http://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/) and contains the Aqua AIRS and 1km 
resolution MODIS matchup indices.  An example ncdump from the following matchup file will 
serve to explain its format. The index file for AIRS Granule 162 on Nov. 02, 2015 is 
IND_AIRS_MODIS1km_AIRS.2017.10.21.192.nc. 

{ 
dimensions: 

GranuleCount_ImagerPixel = 1522881 ; 
sounder_atrack = 135 ; 
sounder_xtrack = 90 ; 

variables: 

int number_of_pixels(GranuleCount_ImagerPixel) ; 

      number_of_pixels:long_name = "imager cross-track index" ; 
      number_of_pixels:units = "none" ; 
      number_of_pixels:var_desc = "imager cross-track index" ; 

int FOVCount_ImagerPixel(sounder_atrack, sounder_xtrack) ; 

      FOVCount_ImagerPixel:long_name = "count of imager pixels per sounder FOV" ; 
      FOVCount_ImagerPixel:units = "none" ; 
      FOVCount_ImagerPixel:var_desc = "count of imager pixels per sounder FOV" ; 

int number_of_lines(GranuleCount_ImagerPixel) ; 

      number_of_lines:long_name = "imager along-track index after concatenating imager granules 
along track" ; 
      number_of_lines:units = "none" ; 
      number_of_lines:var_desc = "imager along-track index after concatenating imager granules 
along track" ; 

// global attributes: 

:_NCProperties = "version=2,netcdf=4.7.4,hdf5=1.12.0," ; 
:VERSION = "1" ;

There should be 240 files per day, corresponding to 240 AIRS granules. Global attribute 
AIRS_file_name gives the L1b AIRS file that collocation is performed for. Global attribute MODIS 
file names give the MODIS geolocation product files that have overlapped observation time 
with the AIRS granule. The MODIS granules are concatenated and cross-track/along-track 
MODIS indices are given based on the dimension of concatenated MODIS granules.  

2.3 Key Science Data Fields 

2.3.1 Science Area 1: Atmospheric Moist Processes and Trends, 

Atmospheric Energy and Water Budget 

The matchup indices could be used to merge and collocate the various sounder and imager L1 
and L2 products to each other. Yue et al. (2022) shows examples of using the index products to 
collocate cloud retrievals from sounders and imagers on Aqua and SNPP to evaluate the 
consistency of cloud products from different instruments across different satellite platforms. 

Yue et al. (2016) used the collocated AIRS and MODIS indices to study the radiative sensitivity 
of clouds at the top of the atmosphere from A-Train observations. Observational-based cloud 
radiative kernels were derived to study interannual cloud feedback using satellite observations 
(Yue et al. 2019). 

The AIRS capability in resolving marine boundary layer thermodynamic vertical structure is 
quantified in Yue et al. (2011 and 2013). 

2.3.2 Science Area 2: Satellite Intercalibration and Cross-Validation 
The sampling biases of the infrared sounder retrieved temperature and water vapor profiles are 
evaluated using collocated sounder-imager matchup index and geophysical variables (Wong et 

15 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 al. 2015, Yue et al. 2013). The impact of cloud clearing on sounder cloud retrievals is analyzed in 
Yue et al. (2022). 

The thermodynamic phase of the clouds in the Arctic region from multiple A-Train satellites is 
validated by Peterson et al. (2020). 

3.0 Data Contents 

Refer to Section 2 for data content. 

4.0 Options for Reading the Data 

4.1 Command Line Utilities 

4.1.1 ncdump 
The ncdump program can be found in the bin directory of the HDF installation area. Consult 
your local computer system administrator for the specifics. 

The ncdump tool can be used as a simple browser for NetCDF data files, to display the 
dimension names and sizes; variable names, types, and shapes; attribute names and values; 
and optionally, the values of data for all variables or selected variables in a NetCDF file. The 
most common use of ncdump is with the –h option, in which only the header information is 
displayed. 

ncdump [-c|-h] [-v ...] [[-b|-f] [c|f]] [-l len] [-n name] [-d n[,n]] filename 

Options/Arguments: 

[-c] Coordinate variable data and header information 

[-h] Header information only, no data 

[-v var1[,...]] Data for variable(s) <var1>,... only data 

[-f [c|f]] Full annotations for C or Fortran indices in data 

[-l len] Line length maximum in data section (default 80) 

16 

 
 
 [-n name] Name for netCDF (default derived from file name) 

[-d n[,n]] Approximate floating-point values with less precision filename File name of input 
netCDF file 

Note: the ncdump tool will only display variables whose ranks are great than 1. In other words, 
you will not see one dimensional vectors such as satheight using this tool. 

4.2 Tools/Programming 

4.2.1 IDL and Python tools to read and use the matchup indices 
Figure 1 shows the location of collocated SNPP VIIRS 750m pixels on 9 CrIS FOVs (corresponding 
to one ATMS FOR) from SNPP CrIS Granule 002 on Jan. 15, 2015. 

Figure 1: Example of sounder and imager 
collocation. Here location of collocated SNPP 
VIIRS 750m pixels on 9 CrIS FOVs 
(corresponding to one ATMS FOR) from SNPP 
CrIS Granule 002 on Jan. 15, 2015 are shown. 

Tools to read and analyze the JPL WVCC index products are available from 
https://github.com/JPL-WVCC/wvcc_tools, where examples of IDL and Python scripts are provided 
to show how to read in the index products and merge/collocate different sounder and imager 
L1 and L2 products. SNPP VIIRS and CrIS data are used in this example.

 5.0 Data Services 

GES DISC provides basic temporal and advanced (event) searches and other services that can be 
drilled from the product pages: 

https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/datasets?page=1&keywords=AIRS_CPR_IND%20AIRS_CPR_MAT%20
AIRSM_CPR_MAT%20AIRS_MDS_IND  

As the project progresses, more services will be added that will allow options for subsetting, 
format conversion, and previews using open source software such as OPeNDAP and Panoply.

6.0 More Information 

All WVCC data types are published to the Global Change Master Directory: 

http://gcmd.nasa.gov 

This is a centralized depository of climate data information that is cataloged by popular 
keywords, which facilitate data discovery. Since the Directory is visited by a large number of 
people working on a broad range of research topics, it is an excellent forum to popularize data 
collections.
